TextENGNew: This year, we had deployed a sufficient rapid-response system.
As a result, we were able to secure the scene and begin the investigation immediately.
The victim was the wife of the younger brother of the Houjou couple.
As a target for the fourth year of Oyashiro-sama's curse, she was an exceptionally fitting choice.
Her reputation among the neighbors was abysmal.
After the incident, everyone said she had only gotten what she deserved.
...I already had a strong idea of who the culprit was.
Most likely... Satoshi Houjou.
That guileless-looking boy.
He had a motive.
The deceased had relentlessly tormented his younger sister.
This had to be an act of retribution.
Furthermore, his behavior when brought to the crime scene for verification was subtly restless, and yet, at certain moments, unnervingly calm in a way no uninvolved person would be.
...My instincts, honed through years of detective work, told me—eight or nine times out of ten, this was the one.
I had no definitive proof tying him to the crime, but... if I brought him in for voluntary questioning and applied a little pressure, I had a feeling he would confess.
"Given how severely the victim was battered, the culprit must have been splashed with a considerable amount of blood.@k@vS20/19/VTK1_kumagai1008. If we can find that, it could be decisive evidence."

"Well, unless he's a complete fool, he's surely disposed of it already.@k@vS20/11/VTK1_oishi1082. But if he confesses and we can recover evidence from the location he claims to have dumped it, that should satisfy the prosecutor."
Taken in isolation, this was a rather straightforward case.
...If Satoshi Houjou confessed, it would be closed.
Everyone but me probably thought so.
...But viewed as part of the series of mysterious deaths, the case was far from over.
Behind this incident lurked the inner workings of the village—the Three Families, or perhaps the Sonozaki family directly.
changeReason: Old translation omitted 村の深部 (the inner depths/workings of the village) which frames the conspiracy as embedded in the village's core structure; new translation includes this context.

TextENGNew: If they were the masterminds, they must have manipulated Satoshi Houjou into committing the crime as their instrument.
......But if that were the case, then Satoshi Houjou would be someone who knew too much.
The Sonozaki family operated solely from the shadows.
Would they really leave someone alive who could become the key to exposing them...?
They had exploited Satoshi Houjou's devotion to his sister, goading him into killing the aunt and making him the executor of the fourth year's curse...
And then, once they eliminated Satoshi Houjou—the one person who could connect them to the crime...
......Satoshi Houjou was no ordinary criminal.
...He may well have been a puppet, set up and discarded.
changeReason: Old translation stated definitively 'He was set up' but Japanese uses かもしれない (might have been) and includes 道化 (puppet/fool); new translation preserves both the uncertainty and the metaphor.

TextENGNew: And after that... my suspicions proved correct.
...He vanished without a trace.
And the manner of his disappearance was puzzling.
He did not flee because he feared his crime would be discovered.
......Another culprit had surfaced.
A man already arrested for a different offense, who had moreover died in prison.
He had confessed to details only the true culprit could have known.
Therefore—he was declared the culprit.
changeReason: Old translation said 'he must be the culprit' (expressing uncertainty) but the Japanese ゆえに犯人 states it as a conclusion/ruling; new translation's 'was declared the culprit' better captures the bureaucratic finality Ooishi is describing.

TextENGNew: And since he was already dead, the investigation was closed.
Then, a few days after Watanagashi, Satoshi Houjou disappeared.
......It was obvious that someone who did not want Satoshi Houjou connected to this case was working behind the scenes.
My superiors pressured me to close the investigation, and Satoshi Houjou's disappearance was reclassified as a separate case@k and handed off to an entirely different division.
changeReason: Old translation said 'my boss' (singular) but Japanese says 上層部 (upper management/superiors, plural), implying institutional pressure rather than one person's decision; new translation uses 'my superiors.'

TextENGNew: The hurried manner in which this case was being closed@k bore a striking resemblance to the way the Houjou couple's fatal fall two years ago had been forcibly ruled an accident.
changeReason: Old translation omitted the key detail that the previous case had been 強引に否定 (forcibly denied criminal involvement) and 無理やり事故ということで決着 (forcefully concluded as an accident); new translation includes these details about the cover-up.

TextENGNew: ......There was no doubt this was the work of "someone" with the power to pressure the police from above.
And ironically... in the Hinamizawa area, the only entity capable of exerting such influence over the police was the Sonozaki family.
...They may have thought they were covering their tracks, but in doing so, they had revealed themselves more clearly than ever.
According to my informant, Oryou Sonozaki had mentioned the fourth year's curse at a family meeting and hinted that she was the one orchestrating it.
...Hints alone did not constitute evidence, but there was no mistaking that Oryou Sonozaki sat at the very top of this conspiracy...
I know who killed the old man.
And yet, I just cannot get my hands on that tail...!
changeReason: Old translation omitted the 尻尾 (tail) metaphor which refers to catching evidence of wrongdoing; new translation preserves this Japanese idiom of 'grabbing the tail' (catching someone in the act).

TextENGNew: The fugitive ringleader from the dam case was still on the run, undoubtedly being sheltered somewhere by the Sonozaki family.
...I would track him down, and I would find the old man's missing right arm.
I would return that arm to his grave... and force both the ringleader and the masterminds to prostrate themselves before it.
...Of course, just as Satoshi Houjou had vanished, the ringleader was likely already eliminated.
He had probably been killed long ago and buried somewhere he would never be found.
But if that was the case, then so be it.
......I would make the old hag who gave the order to kill him grind her forehead into the dirt before his grave.
...This, I swear...!!
With the curse of Oyashiro-sama now in its fourth year, there was already speculation—premature as it was—that something would surely happen again in the fifth year.
Despite all my preparations, I had been unable to make productive use of this fourth occurrence.
changeReason: Old says 'couldn't prevent the incident' (failure to stop it) while JPN says 'couldn't make productive use of this fourth occurrence' (failure to leverage it for investigation). Different characterization of what failed.

TextENGNew: Reacting after the fact was simply not enough.
...I had to keep watch on the inner workings of the village before the next incident occurred...
Moreover, there was a pressing reason I had to bring this to a resolution by next year.
I was reaching... my mandatory retirement.
changeReason: Old translation said 'I'm retiring' but Japanese says 定年を迎える (reaching mandatory retirement age); new translation specifies 'mandatory retirement' which is a key distinction—Ooishi isn't choosing to retire.

TextENGNew: My mother was adamant about returning to her hometown in Hokkaido, and had been saying for years that she wanted to move there when I retired.
I saw it as the last act of filial duty I could perform, and had agreed to relocate to Hokkaido upon retirement.
...That meant next year was my final chance to avenge the old man.
This had happened four years running.
...In all likelihood, there would be a fifth year as well.
The series of mysterious deaths had only tightened the bonds of village solidarity that had been forged during the dam protests.
...Because it had been made unmistakably clear that traitors would face retribution in the name of the curse.
...For the masterminds, there was more than sufficient reason to continue the series of mysterious deaths.
According to rumors, this year's victim was cursed because she had impiously refused to attend the Watanagashi festival.
changeReason: Old translation omitted 不信心 (impiety/irreligion) as the reason; new translation includes 'impiously refused' which captures the religious dimension of the village's interpretation.

TextENGNew: Consequently, some villagers now believed that if they skipped next year's festival, they might become the next target of the curse.
......Next year's festival was certain to draw an enormous crowd.
It would happen again next year, without a doubt.
changeReason: Old translation's 'Something will happen' omitted both 必ず (definitely/without a doubt) and 来年 (next year); new translation includes the full conviction and timeframe.

TextENGNew: The Hinamizawa Village Serial Mysterious Deaths.
Commonly known as the Curse of Oyashiro-sama.
...That would be where I settle everything.
changeReason: Old translation added 'avenge the old man' which isn't in this line (Japanese just says そこでこそ必ず = 'there, without fail'); the revenge vow appears in the next line. New translation is more faithful to the trailing structure.

TextENGNew: I would avenge the old man...
changeReason: Old translation omitted おやっさんの仇を討つ (avenge the old man) and substituted vague emphasis; new translation includes the actual vow.
